title: Betrayal at House on the Hill (3rd Edition)

subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: Components listed in the provided 3rd Edition rulebook excerpt: 6 double-sided character sheets, 2 haunt books (Secrets of Survival and Traitor’s Tome), 6 character miniatures, 6 miniature bases, 4 sliders per player for traits, 74 game cards total (Event, Item, and Omen), room tiles including 3 starting tiles (the large ground-floor triple start tile, Basement Landing, and Upper Landing) plus additional room tiles in a shuffled deck, 5 scenario cards, 5 player reference cards, 1 traitor reference card, 1 monster reference card, 2 dice, 1 tracker with indicator, and 114 cardboard tokens. The component panel in the excerpt also shows room-tile and token count groupings; all setup and play references in this guide follow those listed totals and item types.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: 1) Set both haunt books aside; they are not used until the haunt begins. 2) Place the three starting tiles in the center area, separated as instructed (about 20–25 cm apart): the large ground-floor start tile and the two landing tiles for basement and upper floor. 3) Shuffle the remaining room tiles into a face-down stack. 4) Separate Event, Item, and Omen cards into three decks and shuffle each; place face down within reach of all players. 5) Put the monster reference card and traitor reference card face up on the table. 6) Set the tracker, indicator, and cardboard tokens aside for later use when required by haunt setup.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Each player chooses one character and takes the matching character sheet, miniature, and color base, then inserts miniature into base. Each player receives four trait sliders and sets them to the green starting values shown on their character sheet (for Might, Speed, Knowledge, and Sanity). Each player receives one player reference card. All explorers begin on the Entrance Hall space of the large starting tile.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: As a group, choose one of the 5 scenario cards. That scenario establishes why the explorers entered the house and determines which haunt branch is tied to this game. Return unused scenario cards to the box. Determine first player by birthday rule from the excerpt: the player whose character has the next upcoming birthday goes first. Play proceeds clockwise. The game begins in the pre-haunt phase with all players on the same side.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content: Before the haunt, use this turn flow: 1) Start turn with available movement based on your current Speed and effects. 2) Move through discovered rooms and/or reveal one new adjacent room tile when exploring unknown space. 3) Resolve the newly revealed tile and draw/resolve a card if the room shows Event, Item, or Omen symbol. 4) If an Omen is discovered, make a haunt roll under 3rd Edition rules (roll one die per discovered Omen; if any die shows 5+ threshold condition from rules excerpt, the haunt starts; if haunt still has not started when the last Omen is drawn, it starts automatically). 5) End turn. In this edition, a turn ends automatically when you discover/place a new room tile and resolve it, unless another explicit effect extends your actions. After haunt begins, follow haunt-specific turn and objective rules while maintaining core movement/combat/check framework unless the haunt overrides it.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Core pre-haunt actions and options from the provided rules sections: 

Movement: Move through adjacent connected rooms according to your current Speed and tile links. Forced movement effects can override normal movement choice. 

Exploration: When you move into unexplored edge space, reveal and place an eligible room tile from the room stack. Tile placement grows the house differently each game. 

Card resolution: If a room displays Event, Item, or Omen symbol, draw the matching card deck and resolve its text immediately. Events are usually one-time effects; Items grant ongoing capabilities; Omens are dangerous signs that also influence haunt timing. 

Checks/tests: Many card and room effects call for trait checks (Might, Speed, Knowledge, Sanity). Roll the specified dice and compare against target/effect text. 

Trait management: Gain, lose, and heal traits by moving sliders as instructed. If a critical trait falls to fatal level, your explorer dies (corpse remains on tile in this edition). 

Trading: Players may exchange permitted items/powers according to turn and haunt rules when conditions are met. 

Special actions: Certain cards and haunts grant explicit special actions; these can break normal patterns but must be read and followed exactly.

Post-haunt information rule: during haunt, if you perform an action from your haunt book, opponents may ask you to read the full action text aloud; once used, that action information is no longer secret.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
Two-phase game structure: Betrayal 3e has a cooperative exploration phase followed by a haunt phase where teams/objectives shift. In many haunts, one player becomes traitor; in others, there may be no traitor, hidden traitor, or free-for-all structures. 

Scenario-card-driven haunt selection: unlike earlier matrix-based selection, the chosen scenario card sets the haunt path and traitor determination logic. 

Haunt trigger update: the haunt roll procedure changed in 3rd Edition (one die per discovered Omen; haunt starts when threshold result occurs; automatic trigger if last Omen appears first). 

Turn-ending change: in this edition, discovering and resolving a new room tile ends your turn by default. 

No default stealing: stealing items after combat is not a baseline rule in this edition unless a haunt explicitly grants it. 

Corpse looting: dead explorers leave a body on the room where they died; other explorers can take items and omens from that body, and some haunts use corpses for objectives or powers. 

Open action information during haunt: when an action is used from a haunt book, its text can be demanded and read openly, reducing rules ambiguity and hidden procedural advantages.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content: Betrayal does not use victory points. The game ends immediately when one side fulfills its haunt-specific win condition, as written in the corresponding hero and traitor haunt sections. During setup of the haunt, both sides read their introductory/setup text aloud before separating, then continue with side-specific hidden instructions. Depending on the haunt, winners may be heroes, traitor, hidden side, or an individual in free-for-all format. The provided rulebook excerpt defines this as objective completion rather than score totals; therefore there are no universal VP sources and no universal tie-breaker. If a timing conflict appears, resolve by haunt text priority first, then group consensus as recommended by the rules’ play-smoothly guidance.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: 1) Explore efficiently but keep relative team spacing. Splitting too far in pre-haunt can leave weak characters isolated when betrayal starts. 2) Build broad capability across the table: try to distribute key Items and Omens so one unlucky role flip does not remove all power from one side. 3) Track trait health early—especially low Speed and low Might characters—because random losses can put someone at death risk before the haunt. 4) Read every card effect carefully and apply immediately; small pre-haunt gains stack into major post-haunt survivability. 5) Use scenario awareness: because scenario card influences haunt path, plan with uncertainty but remember that the game is designed for a dramatic role split, not pure co-op optimization.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Misplaying 3rd Edition haunt timing with old-edition memory. If your group uses the wrong haunt roll logic, game balance breaks fast. 
2) Ignoring the automatic end-of-turn on room discovery in 3e, which creates illegal extra actions and tempo mistakes. 
3) Hoarding all useful gear on one explorer. If that explorer dies or becomes traitor, the team can collapse. 
4) Not resetting mindset when haunt begins. Players sometimes keep acting co-op even after objectives diverge; this hands wins to the opposing side. 
5) Skimming haunt setup text. Most losses in Betrayal come from missing one line of setup, component placement, or action restriction.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
Pace of exploration vs safety: exploring quickly accelerates card gain and map knowledge, but also accelerates omen count and haunt trigger. 

Positioning vs efficiency: grouping explorers reduces risk and supports trades, while spreading increases map reveal and card opportunities. 

Resource concentration vs resilience: stacking strong items on one character creates tactical spikes, but distributing tools across explorers protects you against betrayal and deaths.

metadata.notes: Primary source used: provided Betrayal at House on the Hill 3rd Edition rulebook excerpt (French).
